actionable_predicate: Maintain a quarterly nine-property contract audit for the company's load-bearing trunk contracts (typically 10-30 contracts at most companies). Each contract is scored against the nine properties; the audit produces a list of contracts that actually meet the spec versus contracts that are substrate-coupled. The audit informs roadmap conviction: which contracts can the company invest in vs. which are stop-gaps?
example_decision: Q3 fintech audit. 32 contracts reviewed. /v1/charge passes all nine; /v1/balance fails unwettable (substrate leakage) and stable (silent field addition). PM-side roadmap: invest engineering-quality bandwidth in the 16 trunk-contracts that pass all nine; explicitly mark the 9 substrate-coupled ones as 'tactical' and avoid investing in their durability. The remaining 7 are flagged for remediation owners with named timelines. The nine-property audit is the artefact PM uses to push back on 'we need to add this temporary contract' requests — temporary contracts are fine if labelled, but they don't get the trunk's investment.
objection: The nine-property audit is itself work. The verse correctly carves out (in the counter-example) that not every interface needs the audit; the audit is for the load-bearing trunk. The reality-test framing is what makes this PM-actionable rather than aspirational. PASS.
